rulings.Case Title: FEDERAL INSURANCE COMPANY VS. CHRONOMITE LABORATORIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Leave To File A X-Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, June 13, 2019, Line 2, CROSS DEFENDANT CROWN ENERGY SERVICES, INC.'S Motion For Leave To File A X-Complaint Cross-Defendant Crown Energy Services, Inc. dba Able Engineering Services' motion for leave to file a cross-complaint is granted. The court "shall grant" leave to file a cross-complaint "at any time during the course of the action." (Code Civ. Proc. § 426.50; see Silver Organizations Ltd. v. Frank (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d 94, 98-99 ["A motion to file a cross-complaint at any time during the course of the action must be granted unless bad faith of the moving party is demonstrate where forfeiture would otherwise result."].) Defendant Chronomite Laboratories, Inc. has not shown that Crown is acting in bad faith. If Chronomite wishes to challenge the viability of the proposed cross-complaint, it may do so by way of demurrer or motion for summary judgment. (See Brown v. Ralphs Grocery Co. (2018) 28 Cal.App.5th 824, 843 ["The 'better course of action' is to allow a plaintiff to amend the complaint 'and then let the parties test its legal sufficiency in other appropriate proceedings.' [Citation.] Thus, a defendant is not required to make its arguments about the sufficiency of the pleading in an opposition to a motion for leave to amend."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Crown is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JAGPREET SUMMAN VS. PIER 39 STUDIO MANHATTAN, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Terminating Or, In The Alternative, Issue And Evidence Sanctions; To Deem Certain Facts Admitted; And For Monetary Sanctions Pursuant To Ccp Sections 2023.010, 2023.020, 2023.030, 2033.280 And Cal. Rule Of Court 2.30 (B) And (D)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, June 13, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT STUDIO SF ACCESSORIES, INC.'s Motion For Terminating Or, In The Alternative, Issue And Evidence Sanctions; To Deem Certain Facts Admitted; And For Monetary Sanctions Pursuant To Ccp Sections 2023.010, 2023.020, 2023.030, 2033.280 And Cal. Rule Of Court 2.30 (B) And (D) Pro Tem Judge Robert Kane, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Per the office of counsel for defendant and moving party, the motion set forth June 13, 2019 was refiled and is to now scheduled to be heard on June 17, 2019. Therefore, the hearing set for June 13th is off calendar. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rkane1089@aol.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JULIE COHEN VS. BANK OF THE WEST, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Action
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, June 13, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT BANK OF THE WEST, INC's Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Action (part 1 of 2) Defendant Bank of the West, Inc.'s motion to compel arbitration and stay action is granted. By the terms of the arbitration provision in the Contingent Worker Agreement, Bank of the West is an intended third-party beneficiary of the Agreement. (See Agreement 8 ["By signing this Agreement you consent and agree that any dispute that arises out of or relates to your assignment with the Bank (including, without limitation, any dispute with or claim against the Bank), . . . shall be submitted to binding arbitration[.]" (emphasis added)]; id. 8(ii) ["Bank may lawfully seek enforcement of this Section of the Contingent Worker Agreement under the Federal Arbitration Act . . . to the fullest extent allowed by law."].) Therefore, Bank of the West may enforce the agreement to compel Plaintiff Julie Cohen's claims to arbitration. (See Jenks v. DLA Piper Rudnick Gray Cary US LLP (2015) 243 Cal.App.4th 1, 8 [holding that third-party beneficiaries have standing to compel arbitration even if they are not signatories to the arbitration agreement]; Cione v. Foresters Equity Services, Inc. (1997) 58 Cal.App.4th 625, 638-640 [holding that an employer could enforce an arbitration agreement as a third-party beneficiary notwithstanding the fact that it had a separate integrated employment agreement with plaintiff that did not have an arbitration clause].) Even after Plaintiff's voluntary dismissal of her sixth through eleventh causes of action, Plaintiff's complaint still contains allegations relating to the period from April to October 2017 when she was employed by Matrix Resources, including a claim of gender discrimination dating to April 2017 that forms a basis for her first and twelfth causes of action. (E.g., Complaint 7-9.) Plaintiff also refers to Matrix and Bank of the West collectively as Defendants throughout the complaint and alleges that each acted as the other's agent. (Id. 4,5.) The result is that Plaintiff's complaint as currently pled makes allegations of discrimination related to her time as an employee of Matrix that are intertwined with later allegations from her employment at Bank of the West. While the majority of the allegations relate to the latter, the arbitration clause is written broadly to encompass any dispute that arises out of or relates to her assignment with Bank of the West, and all doubts about whether claims fall within the scope of the arbitration provision must be resolved in favor of arbitration. (Agreement 8; AT&T Technologies, Inc. v. Communications Workers of America (1986) 475 U.S. 643, 650.) Because the arbitration agreement is between Matrix and Plaintiff, it does not constitute a prior agreement between Bank of the West and Plaintiff. As a result, the Offer Letter and other employment documents Plaintiff signed when she officially became an employee of Bank of the West do not supersede the arbitration agreement. (See Offer Letter at p. 2 ["[T]he provisions of this offer letter supersede any prior representations or agreements that might have been made between Bank of the West and myself."] [emphasis added].) (end of tentative ruling part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JULIE COHEN VS. BANK OF THE WEST, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Bank Of The West, Inc. Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Action (Tentative Ruling Only Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, June 13, 2019, Line 4, Defendant Bank Of The West, Inc. Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Action (Tentative Ruling Only Part 2 Of 2) Finally, Plaintiff has not shown that the arbitration agreement is permeated with unconscionability. Even though the arbitration agreement is a contract of adhesion, "that finding, standing alone, is not sufficient" to find unconscionability bars enforcement. (Diaz v. Sohnen Enters. (2019) 34 Cal.App.5th 126, 132.) The entire Agreement is only six pages and the arbitration section itself is just over one page. Nor is failure to provide a copy of the AAA Rules sufficient to find procedural unconscionability. Because "[p]laintiff does not claim anything was hidden in the AAA rules . . . the failure to attach the applicable AAA rules did not increase the procedural unconscionability of the application or its arbitration provision." (Nguyen v. Applied Medical Resources Corp. (2016) 4 Cal.App.5th 232, 249; see also Lane v. Francis Capital Management LLC (2014) 224 Cal.App.4th 676, 691-692 [employer's failure to attach a copy of AAA's then current Employment Arbitration Rules and Mediation Procedures to the arbitration agreement did not render the agreement procedurally unconscionable; "There could be no surprise, as the arbitration rules referenced in the agreement were easily accessible to the parties-the AAA rules are available on the Internet."].) At most, any procedural unconscionability was slight. There is also minimal substantive unconscionability. The AAA Rules provide for discovery at Section 9 and the Agreement does not place any restrictions on discovery. Moreover, the AAA Rules do not impose costs in excess of the costs of bringing these claims in Court. However, because FEHA only provides for attorneys' fees and costs for defendants where they can show the plaintiff's claim is frivolous, the prevailing party cost and fee provision in the Agreement is severed to the extent it is inconsistent with this policy. (See Agreement 8(iv) ["[T]he arbitrator shall have discretion to award the prevailing party reasonable costs and attorney fees incurred in bringing or defending an action under this Section, to the fullest extent allowed by law . . ."]; Gov. Code § 12965 ["[A] prevailing defendant shall not be awarded fees and costs unless the court finds the action was frivolous, unreasonable, or groundless when brought, or the plaintiff continued to litigate after it clearly became so."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Bank of the West is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of tenataive ruling part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn To Compel Production Of Documents
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, June 13, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANT ELIZABETH PHILLIPS' Motion To Compel Production Of Documents Pro Tem Judge Robert Kane, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Denied without prejudice as no formal Request for Production of Documents was made in accordance with Code of Civil Procedure section 2031.010 et seq. While informal discovery is allowed, failure to comply does not result in court intervention. (See Pullin v. Superior Court (2000) 81 Cal.App.4th 1161.) Also no courtesy copy of the motion to compel was delivered to Department 301 as required by Local Rule 2.7(B). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rkane1089@aol.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: *** TRANSFERRED TO LOS ANGELES SUPERIOR COURT *** POUR GUYS SOMA LLC VS. WENDY O'CONNOR, L.M.F.T. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Change Venue
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, June 13, 2019, Line 11 DEFENDANT BRANDY COHEN-BROWN, D.O. Notice Of Motion To Change Venue Defendant Brandy Cohen-Brown, D.O.'s motion to change venue is granted. The case is transferred to be heard in Los Angeles Superior Court. Plaintiff shall pay the transfer fees. "[T]he county where the defendants or some of them reside at the commencement of the action is the proper court for the trial of the action." (Code Civ. Proc. § 395(a).) Both Defendants are residents of Los Angeles. Venue is only proper where the injury occurred when the injury is a physical injury to a person or personal property; the injury "does not include mere injury to reputation, business or personal feelings." (Plum v. Newhart (1931) 118 Cal.App. 73, 75.) Therefore, the relevant injury is not the injury to Plaintiff Pour Guys Soma LLC in being subjected to Jason Roussos' lawsuit. Rather, the relevant injury is the alleged injury to Mr. Roussos stemming from alleged misdiagnosis and mistreatment, which occurred in Los Angeles. Furthermore, "nothing in the law provides for venue to be determined at the commencement of the action based on the convenience of plaintiff's witnesses." (Cholakian & Assocs. v. Superior Court (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 361, 373.) Plaintiff has not met its burden to show that a transfer of venue on that ground is warranted. In any event, Plaintiff does not need thirty-seven firefighters, doctors, police officers, and other witnesses to the underlying assault to determine whether Defendants violated their standard of care in treating Mr. Roussos. Plaintiff can arrange to have any key witnesses, such as its one expert witness, transported to Los Angeles. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

Superior Court (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 361, 373.) Plaintiff has not met its burden to show that a transfer of venue on that ground is warranted. In any event, Plaintiff does not need thirty-seven firefighters, doctors, police officers, and other witnesses to the underlying assault to determine whether Defendants violated their standard of care in treating Mr. Roussos. Plaintiff can arrange to have any key witnesses, such as its one expert witness, transported to Los Angeles.
